speaker: interviewer
text: Edith, you were telling us about getting a-- another-- getting false-- other false papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So I got another ID card, the name of Olszewskiej, Jadwigi Olszewskiej. And just as I was coming out from this office, it was right near the railroad station, I-- all of a sudden, I was caught by a Polish policeman. And he took me by the-- by my elbow, and took me into one of those big entrances of the apartment building. In Poland, you have many courtyards. It's like one building behind the other. And he took me in there. And he said, your identity card, please. And I-- I had a-- it was in the winter, so I had a muff. And I had both cards, the new one and the old one, in that muff. And, you know, so I pulled one out. They were gray paper and they were, like, folded. And all the information was on the inside of that folded thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, I took one out. I had no-- and the minute I took it out, he took it from me. And-- and I had no idea which one he had, if he had the one on [NON-ENGLISH] or the one on Olszewskiej. So-- and he stood in front of me, like a foot or two feet at front of me. And he opened it up and he looks at it. And he says, what's your name? And he-- I didn't know, [NON-ENGLISH] or Olszewskiej. But I-- luckily, I quickly jump around him, behind him, over-- and looking down from over his shoulder. I said, it's written right here. Can't you see? I didn't know that they employ illiterates at the police force. I mean, I just, you know, went on the attack.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that gave me enough time to look for-- at the name. And he had the good one. He had the Olszewskiej. And then he started to question me. But I really didn't do too well because, I mean, I had remembered or where I was born, you know, that was all new to me, the places-- place of birth and birth date. But-- and that was the first time that I was caught, you know, like, on the outside. So I was scared. But-- and then he asked me, how do you earn your living. And I didn't-- at that time, I didn't work yet for the railroad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so I said, well, I give German lessons. I teach German. So, how come you know German? And then he became very suspicious.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because [NON-ENGLISH] was born in Silesia, where-- where I was really born, so I knew-- so everybody spoke German. But this other Olszewskiej, she was from-- from eastern Poland, where people didn't speak German. They spoke, maybe Ukrainian or Russian, but not German.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he became suspicious. And then he said, you come with me. And he grabbed me by the-- gave me back the-- the ID card. And I had it in my hand. And he held me by my shoulder. And just come with me. So I didn't know if he was going to take me to the police station. And there, I possibly might-- you know, pay a lot. And I had more money with me. Because I was going for this other ID card, and I thought in case I have some trouble, maybe I be able to pay somebody off. So I had some more money than I would have had.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- or he would take me to the Gestapo. And wherever they were taking me, they would search me and they find the other ID card. So I-- and I didn't know if-- if they start torturing me, if I wouldn't tell where I got the ID card. So I was scared. And I really thought, well, you know, being caught with two ID cards is about the worst that-- that can happen to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I looked when-- as we came out of this building. Right across the street was the Polish and the main railroad station in Warsaw. And there was always an MP. And the MP was going back and forth, back and forth. So I got this bright idea. I shook myself loose. I ran across the street, straight to the MP. And I told him with this ID card, I say-- I said, could you please look at it and see if it looks OK to you? I don't know what this-- what this policeman wants from me. And I spoke in German. So he-- This was a German MP?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a German MP.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he looked at that card. And he says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]-- everything seems to be OK. And, I mean, he didn't know a false Hamburg ID card from a good one. I mean, it just had all these stamps and my picture on it, or [INAUDIBLE]. And I spoke to him and so-- so-- and I said, I don't know what-- with this policeman, what he wants from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he calls this policeman over. And the policeman stands there with his mouth open. And he calls him over. And he slaps him right in the face and say, you stupid so and so. Look for Jews and bandits and leave pretty girls alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to me, he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]-- you know, I should go. And I said, I'm in-- in-- in a-- in a hurry to catch a train. So I ran to the train. And so that was my, you know, my first experience and my first experience with real, you know, chutzpah, that really saved me. And then I had several other experiences. At one point, my-- I was taking a train-- oh. Well, I should go back to my work. I worked at the railroad. I was translating different directives. But I was-- this stationmaster liked me. And I gained his confidence. So he put me in charge of-- of food stamps because the-- the railroad people were getting supplementary food stamps. And so he put me in charge of that. And this station was in charge of some other substations, some smaller-- so that we had quite a number of employees. And I immediately started to steal some of these additional food stamps. And I made, like, two lists of employees. And they didn't check. I would just request it, you know, from the main-- main station. I don't know where it-- don't remember where it was. I need so many, for so many employees, and [LAUGHS] they would just send it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had these additional food stamps. And I also was able to steal a railroad ID card. These people who-- railroad people worked-- were getting special identity cards. So I-- I knew where they were kept.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were actually kept under lock and key. But I would steal-- like, every couple of days, I would steal one or two. And I would give them to the-- to the underground. You know, the person who gave me the-- the first false ID. And they would-- he worked for the underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGHS] What else? I also stole some paper, the official paper with the letterhead, so you could-- and I stamped it in several places. So, you know, if you needed some certificate or something, you could pretend that it was issued by the-- by the German railroad.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- there was a family that was hiding nearby, a Jewish family, that I helped with these food stamps. I also supplied them with the ID cards. And I also stole coal. You know, at that time, we didn't have these electric trains, so we always had coal, and especially at this station. And sometimes I would go on top of the wagon, the-- what's behind the-- well, the-- the locomotive. And I would throw down some coal. And then there was another piece, there was a pile of coal somewhere, I remember. And I would steal that coal and schlep it through a forest to my mother's place. And I put it down in the kitchen. And it was a big heap of coal. And my mother would exchange this coal with the-- with the peasants, for some milk or some eggs. Or sometimes she would sell some of that coal. Because coal was, you know, like gold at that time. And I had one funny experience at one point, but that was a little later, when my-- my parents had to leave that place because of blackmail. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were discovered?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- well, yeah. They were warned that somebody is-- actually, no. It wasn't at that time. But, anyway, let me get to that coal thing. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- two Gestapo man came to the office where I was working. And they ask, is there a [NON-ENGLISH] working for the railroad station. So my-- my boss, the stationmaster, came to me, and says, is there's a [NON-ENGLISH] here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I knew the only [NON-ENGLISH] that had the ID card, the railroad ID card, was my mother. So my heart sank. I thought they caught my mother. And now they want to find out, you know, how she got the ID card or whatnot. And [SIGHS] so you can imagine, you know, I was just dying inside. And I looked through the [INAUDIBLE], the second list. And I looked through that list.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, yes, it-- it's here. She used to work here. But she's not working anymore. Why? What happened to her? So this one Gestapo man says to me, oh, she stole about a ton of coal. And, well, it turned out that really, somebody had sent-- has denounced my parents, and gone to the Gestapo. The Gestapo came to this hut, to the peasant hut, walked in there, saw all that coal. My parents weren't there anymore because they had left for Warsaw. But he asked the peasant, who lived there, you know. And where is this woman? So she-- he said, I don't know where she is. She left. But her sister-- because myself and-- and my mother, we-- we were saying that we were sisters. And he didn't know that my name was Olszewskiej. But her sister works at the railroad. So probably that's how the coal is here. So they assumed that the sister must also be Zoszak. So they came, really, looking for me. But my name was Olszewskiej. But, you know, until he told me that she stole a ton of coal, then I-- I realized what was-- and that the peasant told him that she worked at the railroad station. So now-- so they never really caught her and she never-- but he gave him the name of the-- the Zoszak name. So that was a-- a kind of a close call. Your parents went to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, that-- they had-- some blackmailers, Polish blackmailers came in. And my mother was able to-- to hide my father under-- underground. He hid. But-- and it was early in the morning. So she threw his clothing also in there. But the bed was still warm, from-- because my father-- it was early in the morning. And she had forgotten his gold watch was, like, on a little table next to the bed. And three of these blackmailers came in. And they said they're looking for-- they were told that a communist lives here. Because at that time, it wasn't-- for the Poles, they-- they wouldn't say a-- a Jew, but a communist. You know, that somehow it was more acceptable. And so-- so, my mother says, no, no. He-- you know, he's not. And-- and so on. And then they said to my mother, don't lie. The bed is still warm, you know. And-- and so they went. And there was another trapdoor in the regular cellar from the-- in the kitchen, which wasn't the hole that we dig-- dug out. So they went into that cellar to look, two of them. One stayed behind and told my mother, look, I know that he's here. I-- I saw the watch, the man's watch. You give me the watch and-- and I come back later for the watch. And you put a newspaper on that watch. And say, you give me that watch. I come later for it. Don't tell the other ones. And I'm going to get them away from here. He was a smart cookie. And when these two other guys came up from the cellar, he said, oh, yeah. I just saw somebody was running. Because this hut was in the woods. You know, there were fields and-- I saw somebody running. So they ran out, looking for my father. This guy said he-- well, he took the watch and hid it. But he said he's going to come back later for more money, for saving my father. And that's when my parents-- that was early in the morning. And they were able to escape and go to Warsaw. And they had their hiding place. You know, somebody was hiding-- hid my father in one place. Actually, it was a prostitute that didn't want any money. And she had my father for quite a while, for about a week or more. And then, after that, they got another-- an-- another place in a building on the fifth floor. It was an attic apartment. And my mother had somebody build-- hired somebody to make a-- another wall. It was not an apartment. It was one big room. And she made the wall there and with a small opening. So there were, like, two rooms. And in one room, there was a bed and under-- behind the bed, she dug a hole that led to the attic, the regular attic of the-- of the place. And that's where they moved in. So-- and if somebody walked into the front, they knew that somebody would come in, my mother would push a, like, a big armoire in front of this opening. And my father could be in the other-- in the other part of this-- of this apartment. [SIGHS] And-- but even there, we had a very bad experience at one time. But that was towards the end of the war, and-- I had that before liberation. They-- they-- some blackmailers came. And I was visiting. I was with my parents. The blackmailers came. And they-- it was a horrid night. They stayed the whole night. And they-- then they-- in the morning, they took whatever they could. They-- my mother had a little rug and they took it the rug. And they locked the door from the outside and said that they were going to bring the Gestapo. But first they had to get rid of this stuff that they stole. And they left my father practically naked. Oh, all-- all of us practically naked. And-- so-- and-- and they left. And there was no way out, except, you know, like, jumping from the window. They thought that we couldn't get out. But my mother had made that hole to the attic. And we escaped through that attic. And we hid in the attic for a while while my mother was the only one that had a dress. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: That had--

speaker: interviewee
text: A dress. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: --they took everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Clothing. And matter of fact, the dress that she had, she was like a-- a rag that she used for dusting, or she prepared it for dusting. And she-- she put that on and she ran to some acquaintances. And they supplied us with some clothing. And we took the train, went back to Otwock. And actually, that probably saved our life later. Because soon after that, the-- the Russians marched in. And they stopped at the Vistula River. They didn't go any further. We were liberated end of July of '44, while my-- if we had stayed in Warsaw, we would have gone through the Polish uprising, where many, many, you know, people were-- died. And Jews were killed. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So before that, Edith, you were still working for the railroad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I was working for the railroad. I-- I had some other experiences. At one point, I wanted to save my cousin and my aunt. So I-- I took some extra ID cards and false ID cards. And I took the train to Galicia, near Lwow. And-- but in the train, some Ukrainians suspected me of being Jewish. So they wanted to throw me out of the train. And actually, they did. And the train was just stopping. I don't know if they knew that the train would stop there, or if it was just God's hand. But this train was going very slowly, slowed down. And they threw me out. I rolled down the embankment. And afterwards, they threw the knapsack and-- which I had some extra clothing for this cousin that I wanted to save. And so I rolled down the embankment. They threw out the-- the knapsack. And the train had stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I made my way up the embankment. I went straight to the German car. Because the Germans always traveled separately. So I made my way to the German car.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knocked and some officers opened, you know. I said, and I said, well, could I please join them because there are some Ukrainian guys who are trying to have fun with me. And they saw a young girl. So they thought that maybe they tried to, you know, attack me sexually, or whatever. So they let me stay. I did go to this little town where my cousin was. But she didn't want to leave her parents. So she wouldn't want to-- she didn't come with me. And then I went to Lwow. And I tried to get to my aunt. It was my favorite aunt, my mother's favorite sister. And she-- and I couldn't because they'd just had an action, you know, which is like a selection. And there was a lot of shooting and whatnot. And I couldn't get in. And I-- you know, I didn't know where she was because everything was in a-- in a uproar inside the--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: --that area, in that ghetto, in that area where the Jews were. So I knew I probably couldn't find her anyway, and probably lose my life there, too. So I didn't-- I didn't go in. And I-- I still feel very guilty about it. Later on, my mother-- when I came back, my mother tried. And she couldn't. She came too late. And she met-- she had some-- also some horrendous experiences on-- on the way. A German threw her down some steps and she ran after the train. The train was moving already. And she jumped on the bumper, the end, you know, the connection between cars, or that was the last one. She jump and there was a Polish railroad man who pulled her up. And she sat on this bumper at the end of the train with this Polish railroad man who was holding her. And-- and she had also a big bag that she was carrying to take to her sister. Anyway, she went-- she was bleeding because she had fallen down the steps when this German kicked them. And so she-- she said, in her memoirs, she said she was only praying-- she was sure that she's going to die and fall off. But this train was going through Otwock, where she knew I was and my father was. And she was just praying not to fall off near there, because then we would know that, you know, she was-- fell off the train. But-- and she had some-- quite some experiences. And she finally she made it to Lwow. And a guard, an Ukrainian guard who was there, told her that he knew my aunt. Because she was very beloved there. She was very good to-- anyway. But she was-- that she was on that truck. And when they were taking them out and she was yelling, it's all a mistake. It's all a mistake. I'm going-- somebody's coming to save me. It's all a mistake. But they were all taken outside and they had to dig their own graves. And they were all shot. So that's one thing that, you know, I can never, never forget. I still have that-- that picture of her at-- on that truck, halfway-- half out of her mind, you know, in fear, and yelling, somebody's coming to save me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [SIGHS] I get-- well, [LAUGHS] I don't know. I'm talking so much. But there was one-- one incident with a lot of chutzpah on my part. And I still don't know how I-- how I did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: One time, I was in Warsaw railroad station. And the trains-- I wanted to go to Otwock-- and the trains were full. And no Poles could go in. And I had a book. I think it was [INAUDIBLE] or [INAUDIBLE]. I forgot. In German. I very frequently carried a German book with me, so as to pretend that I am German. And as I said, I sometimes jumped on trolley cars, German trolley cars, if somebody was following me. I-- to lose them, I would jump on a German trolley cart, or I approach a German and ask some stupid question, some direction, so whoever followed me, you know, would throw-- be thrown off track. Anyway, that one day, I-- I had to make the train. I knew my parents would be terribly worried if I didn't get there. And the curfew was coming. So-- and I knew the Germans could only go-- always go. And so there were these two officers who kind of looked at me. They were more middle-aged-- you know, for me, they were middle-aged, around 30. And, so I dropped the book. You know, not a [LAUGHS] handkerchief. But the book. And one of them picked it up and saw it was a German book. So he started to talk to me and I spoke with them. And then the train got there. And no Poles could get in the train, only the Germans. And I held back. And he said, [GERMAN]-- come on, come on. And I said, no, I'm not German. I can't go in. Oh, you know, stupid, and you come as our guest. So I-- I went. So that's how I made the acquaintance with these two guys. Well, one was nicer than the other. But you know, they ask me about how come I'm Polish if I speak German, why I'm not at [INAUDIBLE]. So I came back with this my story about my German mother. And anyway-- but I didn't tell them where I-- where I worked or where I lived, you know. Once we got there, I said goodbye. And-- but this one guy, [SIGHS] his name was Ostrus, he found-- he took the train quite frequently. And he saw me at the railroad station. So he found out where I worked. And he tried to prove how nice the Germans were because in the train I said, no, I-- you know, the Germans aren't so nice. Also I have German blood, and-- you know, I am half Polish. And so-- [SIGHS] so he took the train very early in the morning. And he would leave smoked fish or-- or some kielbasa or something in my drawer, to prove he was nice. And it stank there [LAUGHTER] and I would come in and I have a smoked fish in my desk. So-- there's-- there's more to this story. But I know you probably have to change the--

speaker: interviewer
text: We have another minute. Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Well, that's a long story-- not a long story. So anyway, I-- I knew him. And he gave me his phone number. He was an instructor in some officers' school and said if I ever need him, to call him. And I did. I-- I needed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: There is more to this story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's save it, then for the next tape. Because we're-- we're running out.
